Title: 2024: The movie watchlist year
body:
In 2024, I started replacing my "New year's resolutions" with "The year's goals" instead. Not to stop resolutions and building better habits, but more so to stop stressing over a lack of overnight metamorphosis, where I'm anxious about why am I not **x** already at month number **y**? So if the resolution for 2024 was *to be more mindful and appreciate media, mainly films, more deeply again*, I ended up planning a 12 month plan with at least one movie from my ever-growing watchlist a month, and to earnestly write a thought-out and honest critique of the movie, afterwards. I ended up watching over 30 films throughout the year, with 12 I'm counting as part of the goal per my arbitrary conditions, i.e., I must have already have it on my watchlist before starting this goal **AND** must have been released in 2022 or earlier. For all of the movies, I can honestly say I enjoyed the actual act of watching more than I used to prior to setting this year's goals. I was a more active watcher in the sense I was noticing more of the directorial decisions, subtle acting choices, and especially on the specific things that I liked and not liked about a movie. A full list of the aforementioned 12 movies from my watchlist will be at the table below but first, I want to highlight some notable ones to me: • I started the year off with Ryusuke Hamaguchi's Drive my Car, the Best International Feature in 2021, and a Best picture, nominee that gave off uneasiness and an overwhelming ambiance further amplified by the ~3 hours of movie length and **my** lack of Japanese language skills, but this feeling coming, settling, and going also paralleled with Yusuke's and Misaki's understanding of each other in the film; • Orson Welles' Citizen Kane was a pleasant surprise in that I was actively watching the film without getting distracted from the amount of things I've learned *about* the film over the years. In fact, these only enhanced my watching of it. Another Best Picture nominee that, outside of the dated manner of speaking, had a punchy dialogue that made you follow the story **and** the actors in focus, which is unsurprising for the radio star behind the infamous radio adaptation of "The War of the Worlds," and warranted and rewarded, actively watching it. It even gave you the first ever jump scare in film history just to keep you awake and watching. • A Lightning round (er, bullet/s) of the other Best Picture nominees: ◦ Whiplash was anxiety-inducing in the most sensical way possible; ◦ Greta Gerwig's Little Women took an ensemble cast of big names and made you feel a sense of familiarity, filial longing, loss of innocence, heartbreak, and many more in each of these all-stars' moves, words, and camerawork. It felt earnest and not at all a "Hollywood adaption," so to speak; ◦I watched Dune right before watching Dune pt. 2, and it did great establishing the world-building for the core of pt. 2. haven't watched a genuine 2-parter movie since Back To The Future; (which was actually a 3-parter but parts 1 & 2 were still great on their own :D) ◦Good Will Hunting is a classic that just somehow escaped from me. Robin Williams is always great at underscoring humor before hitting you with a haymaker of a gut punch. • Love Exposure is a stupid and crass movie that I cannot, with a good conscience, recommend to most people. It is weird, nonsensical, and a fever dream of a movie that lasted almost 4 hours long. If you Stephen Chow films, and think it's too weird for you, I would say this might not be for you. That being said, as a big Stephen Chow enjoyer, I had an absolute blast with every one-liner, visual joke, camerawork, visual effect and absurd plot point, which I don't think I could say for any other 4 hour movie. If you got here in this paragraph, I hope you do end up watching it on your own accord and liking it so that you can reach out and tell me about it. lol • A couple of notable movies I watched in 2024 that were outside my conditions: Godzilla Minus One (rel. 2023 - ★★★★★) was a return to form to the best Gojira movies where the stories are about the humans living in Godzilla's vicinity, rather than a monster professional wrestling storyline that tries paint Godzilla as either a babyface(Good Guy) or a heel. (Bad Guy) Soundtrack and VFX were obviously great deserving of its awards and accolades; The Fall Guy(rel. 2024 - ★★★) was a good comedy with better action; everything else was just the "*Bacon*" (kek) It was an ode to stuntpeople in the industry by a long-time stuntperson in the industry, and it felt like it. 🙏

Title: 2026: Extinguishing my Literary Backburner of a Library
body: In lieu of a list of New Year's resolutions in 2024, I started putting my money where my mouth is, when it comes to my claims of being a film buff; I started a "[watchlist year](/other/uid=2024)" where I aimed to tick 12 movies from my ever-growing watchlist.
